item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I generally like format screws, but am I really supposed to dig through the edit history here? That's just a bit too much effort for me to bother with when most other format screws on the site require, at most, viewing the page source.

Besides that, since happy endings are relatively rare on this site, I think the ghost girl actually recovering would have been more unexpected than "but it was fake because the Foundation is corrupt and nothing good ever happens there!", which is how the ending came off to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't understand what's going on, here. The dialogue during the examinationsis klunky, whether that's intentional or not is irrelevant, cuz it still makes for a cringey reading experience. And then the "reveal" at the end was confusing. Ok, so some or even all of the data here may be fabricated. And…? There's not enough here to piece together what actually happened, or what the actual anomaly is supposed to be.

Also, the Foundation has access to wildly improbable sci-fi tech, but it's not magic. Capturing ghosts and turning them back into humans a la that 1990's Casper film is kinda silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the ending went from: ghost-girl murdered a 2nd time -> ghost-girl rehabilitated -> ghost-girl murdered a 2nd-time, murder covered up with rehabilitation story, but automated process figured out the cover-up.
I wonder, was your original intent for the rehabilitation story that it was a cover-up, DjAktus? I preferred thinking that the rehabilitation story was the straight up truth. It was a nice bait and switch. Typically when the foundation does something kind it succeeds for a while then backfires. For the 2nd iteration of ghost girl being kind actually worked out for once, in a believable way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I was like: "The name of the psychiatrist for the ghost kid is Angela Kidwell? Oh, come on! That's a fake name if I ever heard one!"

Then I was like: "Oh, it proably was a fake…"

It makes the reader wonder how much of the article is legit and what parts are farbricated.

Article feels like, there was more of a story here, but there are not enough hints and supplements to pierce it togather.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've got issues with the dialogue. That 8-year-old does not talk like an 8-year-old.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The twist of this story is hidden behind "someone changed this data." I love the idea, but it is executed poorly. To determine what is going on, you have to fiddle with the page's history, which is a long log of (often tiny) changes.

I think this could be fixed with a button saying something like "Access Off-site Backup Dated [before James messed with it]"

As is, it's an inconsistent mystery with a weak hook and little resolution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I was pretty eh on the ending. The plot involving SCP-2996 felt strong while this data corruption stuff seemed tacked on. Then I gave it some thought. The whole article is about SCP-2996 coming to terms with her own death, coming to accept what actually her happened to her and to not feel angry about it. And what's a common theme about stories of acceptance like this? Passing on from this world to the next.

This file is what's left after SCP-2996 passed on. And she's happier now.

That could all be bologna of course, but that possibility hit me hard emotionally. So to that and a good mystery, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This leaves a lot of loose ends, and I don't remember the original version of the article, so here's my train of thought:

So, did the SCP, instead of becoming truly neutralized, accidentally RetGone herself in reaction to the amnestics after finding peace? Or did her finding peace somehow manufacture additional documentation? Was her "Dr. Kidwell" real, or was that a name she overheard and it became an imaginary(?) friend who she believed was her therapist as she worked through her trauma and her belief generated documentation to support it?

… Was she never a ghost at all? If she was a ghost at some point, when did she stop being a ghost, and why? If she's been corporeal for all this time, then would anyone be able to distinguish between her being a ghost or not for the duration of her containment?

Right now I'm leaning towards the "minor reality bender in the vein of a much weaker SCP-239 had a psychotic break in which she believed she was a murdered ghost" explanation. I hope she's doing okay now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whew, love the mystery this one ends on.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright. So here we have a few potential links. The first, as always, is turning the mention of Site-81 into a crosslink back to its dossier, so as to make this article more connected with those also contained at this site. Additionally, the Incorporeal Entity Vacuum Chamber mentioned in the Containment Procs has a blueprint in Reach's BP folder, which can be linked to as a means of illuminating this particular piece of site canon. Finally, probably the most famous ghost skip on the site is SCP-1337, which also makes use of the Incorporeal Class system. This mention can link there, and in turn links 2996 to all the skips 1337 links to as well. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-2996 is to be contained within a standard Incorporeal Entity Vacuum Chamber at Site 81. This chamber is to be fitted with 4 Non-Physical Displacement Neutralizers (nPDN). SCP-2996 may be allowed to request certain items for entertainment.

To this:

SCP-2996 is to be contained within a standard Incorporeal Entity Vacuum Chamber at Site-81. This chamber is to be fitted with 4 Non-Physical Displacement Neutralizers (nPDN). SCP-2996 may be allowed to request certain items for entertainment.

Changing this:

SCP-2996 is a Class II Incorporeal Humanoid Entity, initially discovered in an abandoned home in Nashville, Indiana. SCP-2996 appears to be a young human female of European descent with black hair and blue eyes, and a number of visible wounds across the entire body, including a major gunshot wound to the right eye. Although SCP-2996 is by default an incorporeal entity, the use of nPDN devices has allowed Foundation personnel to "anchor" SCP-2996 into a physical state during examinations. It is currently hypothesized that the use of these devices, along with other aspects of containment, is the source of SCP-2996's deteriorating mental/emotional state.

To this:

SCP-2996 is a Class II Incorporeal Humanoid Entity, initially discovered in an abandoned home in Nashville, Indiana. SCP-2996 appears to be a young human female of European descent with black hair and blue eyes, and a number of visible wounds across the entire body, including a major gunshot wound to the right eye. Although SCP-2996 is by default an incorporeal entity, the use of nPDN devices has allowed Foundation personnel to "anchor" SCP-2996 into a physical state during examinations. It is currently hypothesized that the use of these devices, along with other aspects of containment, is the source of SCP-2996's deteriorating mental/emotional state.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So…is this supposed to be some sort of bizarre format screw where you have to read the edit history to get the full story? Because that seems like a bit of a stretch even for this site. Also it doesn't really fit with the message, since Kidwell is a part of the page. If that's not what's going on, then I'm lost, because it certainly seems like there's something more than "oddly mistaken vengeful spirit gets turned into human girl".

Confused vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this and the original comments confused the heck out of me until I found out I was reading a rewrite. Still, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: you stole all the best elements of my SCP-922 in the rewrite. that's always a good thing. more people should emulate me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the story of this SCP, djKaktus was just writing a sad ghost story, but then he was like "how about happy ending" and then people didn't like that ending so he was like "well why not both" and then it all worked out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After going through the post history and the Wayback machine to see what the earlier comments were talking about, I really like the idea of a changing article like this, but I feel it could be executed a bit better. Maybe make it more obvious that there are past revisions, or even format the page to see the different iterations (something like SCP-2317). A security cover-up is a great concept, as long as you don't actually cover it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I mean it might be possible this ending isn't true. The file seems to be corrupted a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Amazing. Truly amazing. Great story, happy ending. Bravo!

item.title: Re: Classification?
item.content:encoded: No, we can't. Make something up.

item.title: Classification?
item.content:encoded: Can someone explain to me how the classification system of incorpeal beings goes? What can a Class 3 do as compared to a Class 5? Can it even go up to Class 5?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Instead of double posting, which can get very spammy, you should try clicking Edit and adding your new thoughts there.

Just some friendly advice :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If this skip is meta then shouldn't there be a meta tag? Or is there even a need to read the revision log at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What the fuck is going on? It's too inconvenient to do investigative work while mobile browsing. No down vote yet because I want to give this skip s chance, liked it except for that very confusing alert thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have never read the previous versions of this article, so reading the comment thread is… baffling. Not in a way that reflects badly on anyone, just… I find that I don't want to read the earlier versions lest it alter how I feel about this!

I enjoyed the way it went. The story was so strange — and the Foundation seems so reticent to document exactly what they think is going on and you know they suspect something specific — that despite the potential cliche ("j/k this story didn't happen") it stays interesting.

Particularly since I suspect that the article did in fact happen exactly as laid out here, but with omitted mechanisms and details, and then something else happened in between the successful adoption of Samantha Pendleton and the present day. Something to cause the data errors. Something that cannot be seen directly. Most likely something to protect Samantha Pendleton, since her existence is now disbelieved by the Foundation, but… there's no way to really know what.

That question of "how much is missing?" is what makes me like the story here.

Anyway, +100, congrats!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm really glad I went back and reread this one. I could spend a bunch of time dissecting the article once again, but the feeling I get after reading it is enough to convince me to upvote. Nicely creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a meta thing this comes of as better than the sum of its parts. I'm not sure it works that well if you don't choose to go through the revision history, but I did and enjoyed it quite a bit as a result, so this earns a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
just kind of takes out the horror at the end that made it so powerful.

Or for later version, the happy ending that also made it powerful in its own way.

Sure it was a breach of security in excess of what I'd expect for the foundation

I say it breach of security in excess of what I'd expect for UIU. An anomalous being manage to infiltrate the Foundation and gain access with skip is one thing. But to killed 7 medical staffs without raising the alarm, has all the time to operated on skip from start to vivisection (he did it all alone too), on what suppose to be scheduled physical evaluation (as the Dr. said in the next interview "There was a mistake, that was not the doctor who was supposed to perform the exam.", suggest that at very least the skip though it was the scheduled physical evaluation), and manage to escape (ok, maybe he's higher rank of ghost and the gadget doesn't work on him, but still…), that's total another level of incompetent for the Foundation.

That is actually better scenario in my head, the worse is that somehow all of the other medical staffs have no idea what they're suppose to do, neither is the evaluation supervised. And thus nobody object when he start vivisect it,

I think this would prompt O5 Council to hold meeting to discuss about the Foundation's security issue. And in the middle of the meeting, O5-██'s teenage cousin will walk in and return several top secret DVDs (few are missing because his friends really like them) :p .

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Revoked my vote since now it become another unreliable report, without much hint of what actually happen.

Still I don't reverse my vote since this appear to be in process of rewrite, and at least you don't bring back that breach which has the Foudation's security being complete joke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What is this even about now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never mind, figured it out. Click history at the bottom of the page and then click the various "V" links for the different versions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there anywhere to read the previous version of this scp?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just remembered that this got changed. I liked the version with the happy ending better. This just throws in a "THINGS HAVE LIED SPOOKY THINGS ARE HAPPENING" twist that feels expected, and dismantles everything built up prior to it. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of the previous versions ended with a note that started with:

"A rogue, unnamed individual, currently believed to be in connection with the open case of SCP-2996, has infiltrated Site 81 and is assumed responsible for the disappearance of 3 site medical doctors and 4 site nurses. The individual is believed to be an anomalous humanoid entity, likely Class III or above."

I must say, I liked that ending better. Sure it was a breach of security in excess of what I'd expect for the foundation, but it had a lot more weight to it. That ending really hit you, whereas this one just kind of takes out the horror at the end that made it so powerful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvote because the girl is so cute.

Just Kidding. Lets say I get sick of how some people seem to love to potray the Foundation as sadistic asshole that kick the dog for no reason, containing for sake of conteaiment (in contrast to their OC, not that we'll ever see that OC again). So I have soft spot for a piece of work that has the Foundation "actually working" with the skip (I readed about this skip first in TV Tropes), and the rest of it is good enough that I see no reason to take that upvote back.

EDIT: And I just checked the old version. Man, so this used to has diabolus ex machina ending? While that's indeed scary, I also find it potray the Foundation's security as complete joke. Good riddance.

Now, current version does bug me a little that she become a complete normal girl instead of move on to afterlife or, for lack of betetr words, an ex-ghost (and thus remain with the Foundation as test subject). Isn't nPDN be the reason why she has physical body at all? Still, it's better than previous version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, so here's what I think:

Ghostbusters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I would prefer to see existing paranormal gadgets (electric pentacles for example) in place of the technobabble in the containment procedures (for better versimiltude and less strain on the special containment concept) and some more esoteric detail on how the rematerialization was accomplished.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoa, a happy ending. Don't see those very often. Quite an interesting article. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like both versions, but this one is generally better.
It does seem to answer one question: why would a ghost, when corporeal, be vulnerable to tranquilizers? Because when all is said and done, she's a living girl. Perhaps she's a latent (and probably weak) reality bender who was so distraught and mentally ill that she "killed herself" and became a "ghost." When unable to phase through solid objects, she was normally affected by drugs.

One complaint: There should be some note regarding ongoing surveillance. Reformed ghost or inactive reality bender, the Foundation would want to keep tabs on her, if only to know if something traumatic befalls her that might lead to a relapse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The previous entry actually scares me. This makes me feel nothing. The idea of an SCP neutralizing on its own volition is worthy of exploring, but the article hasn't really shown the gradual change of its attitude. Not to mention, how does a incorporeal spirit becomes corporeal all of a sudden? Novote for now, but if the author can add more details to how the SCP changes, I might change my mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually like this better after the update. It gives a rare but enjoyable happy ending and opens up the possibility of anomalies being able to be reversed by extensions of their own nature. Have a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although I do enjoy the rare article with a happy ending, this ending just doesn't work for me1. Psychiatric counseling can do wonders for some people, but I don't see how it can transform a Class II Incorporeal Humanoid Entity (deceased since 1929) back into a non-anomalous, corporeal, living human child. If the 're-corporealization' of SCP-2996 was addressed in the article, I'd be inclined to re-upvote. Reverting to neutral for now.

Footnotes
1. Also, I really liked the previous version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
has vehemently remarked on several occasions that its killer was a close, one James Franklin.

A close…?

item.title: Re: Update!
item.content:encoded:
Eh… I liked it better as a horror article. Vote neutralized.

Edit: Actually, no. Vote reversed. I actively dislike this thing now.

item.title: Re: Update!
item.content:encoded: Aw, you got rid of the really interesting bits…

item.title: Re: Update!
item.content:encoded:
Vote reversed.
Now I actually like this. For some reason, I suspect an anonymized and censored version of this article ended up appearing in internal Foundation journals - it is after all an achievement in dealing with malevolent spirits and I guess, good for staff morale.

item.title: Update!
item.content:encoded:
Update!

After much consideration and the poking and prodding of many chat types, I've completely overhauled this skip. Hope you guys liek it~~~

item.title: Different Date
item.content:encoded: At fourth interview log, its name is 'Psychiatric Evaluation Interview 08/04/2012', but the date in quote box is 'August 8th, 2012'. I think it may need to unity one date…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For me, I really enjoyed the SCP's more 'classical' feel towards the horror tropes, having the black and white picture of the girl crawling down the stairs and being a vengeful spirit, these are normally unpopular due to them being dubbed as 'cliche' by many, but it's pleasant to every now and again see an SCP that takes a classic concept and does something interesting with it.

When I was reading the interview logs of the SCP, to me this was an extremely clever, creative and unique idea, a girl committing suicide and feeling so guilty about it, she would go so far as to convince herself it was murder, and in turn force her to return as a vengeful spirit trying to find the man her mind made up to help her cope with her inner guilt. This don't just make the SCP sympathetic, but it cleverly mixes a supernatural element such as a spirit of vengeance, with a mental disorder brought on by the SCP's own guilt she feels even in death, reminding the reader that she intact was once human, and ended up this way through horrible means, but not to the extent that the article was screaming: 'She's a sympathetic character! feel bad for her!'. The idea of a spirit returning not due to an actual true reason, but because of a mental disorder it couldn't bring itself to believe it had was fantastic.

The problem however for me, was when it was revealed that all of this wasn't the case and she actually WAS sane, and the guy DID exist. If the article had just kept it at the SCP's whole existence being based upon a clever twist of the 'vengeful spirit' trope that would have been great, if the last part was just removed, the SCP for me would significantly improve and really be not just a great SCP, but an extremely creative and original one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What do we actually know about how a ghost "works," though? If it's in the image of a real person who's to say it wouldn't function exactly like one if it were made corporeal?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I could actually see them typing up a report like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for me. I really liked this, and the concept of the foundation being able to de-ghost a ghost makes me smile for some reason. I also like how you managed to give a commonly scary (think jump scare ghost) picture a great deal of humanity, rather than the common "Grawwwr!".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I could say a lot of things about this, but ultimately, it's enough to state that the Ethics Committee is concerned with the conduct of foundation staff and so by definition have nothing to do with an intruder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To the arson, murder, and jaywalking point, being killed in the course of a day is something every Foundation employee signed up for. Everyone with the clearance to know, know that just because your cleaning the toilets, or cooking lunch, there is still ways for you to die in the course of your duties.

Humanoid skips, however, are more then merely our prisoners. We are also charged with protecting them with reasonable limits. The vivisection, is the Ethics Committee's view, unexceptable. Death of Foundation personnel, is acceptable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. Everything that was written was done with the storytelling as its primary intent, but I understand how some of the choices made might not come across as well. I appreciate the commentary either way!

item.title: Staffpost- Open
item.content:encoded: Calm down. If you can't express your points with like, even a veneer of courtesy/decency, perhaps you should wait until you are calm enough to do so instead of posting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Calm down, brother.

The idea is that he filled out the report, yes. I've gotten a number of criticism about the addition of that note, but ultimately, it's there for a reason. If you don't like it, that's certainly within your right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is another of those things which I first went meh, then it started building into something promising… and then it sunk into mehness again.

At first, I was absolutely not a fan of the containment procedures - in my view, made-up technology like this is a deus ex machina that takes away an important aspect of the Foundation universe - the struggle to contain and limit the supernatural through human ingenuity, plus, a few tidbits about fictitious science I already mentioned on Humes.
But, know what, this is not a deadly sin.

Now, it came to the interviews and I loved what you did, and even liked the interplay with the containment procedures - the whole load of speculative stuff was given a lampshade by the confused ghost going "y u no work" and being genuinely surprised it can't do squat.

Then came the end, and I went.. "buh?"
First of all, there's the expunged log. Given what happened in the final breach, there's absolutely no reason to expunge what was logged - to the contrary, the contents might reveal important information about the infiltrator - the assumption is that he came in to do what he did for a purpose, rather than for the laughs. Also, I'm pretty certain "vivisectomy" is not a word.

Then, the final message.
First of all, I'm kinda not buying that an individual like that would be able to infiltrate a Foundation facility and wreak so much havoc outside of very specific circumstances, even if anomalous - especially since this version of the Foundation is advanced enough to make containing ghosts a doddle.
I know stunts like this were pulled IRL, and that there are ways this could have worked (the description of him that exists suggests he took refuge in audacity, which is tricky to pull off but works quite well) , but the way it's presented just makes me go 'nope'.

This individual is also wanted by the Foundation Ethics Committee: performing an illegal vivisection on a conscious, sapient humanoid entity.

Also, this note is extremely murder, arson and jaywalking. Asides from messages like this absolutely totally and utterly not meshing with my perception of the Ethics Committee, which is a lot like Foundation political police, the offence pales in comparison to the presumed murders of seven personnel and most importantly infiltrating a Foundation site to the point that together it sounds silly.

Ultimately, a downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay firstly, even though I upvoted this, that fucking wall- no- that was a whole damn building of black text got me genuinely angry for a second, that was nothing but a fucking waste of time for both you and I.
Second, I do like the reveal, but if the doctor that performed the physical examination wasn't a real doctor, and the vivisection he performed wasn't the planned procedure, then why the FUCK is there even a physical examination report to be blacked out? Did the fucking evil ghost submit the report after he was done murdering Foundation personnel and not giving a fuck in general? Did the Foundation watch the security tape and write up the ghost's sadistic live vivisection that probably had little scientific merit because "shit, when life gives you lemons…"
Christ, almost makes me want to go back and downvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, so whatever killed her the first time infiltrated the Foundation and killed her a second time?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1. I liked the picture, because it really did gear me for a kind of generic jump-scare sort of ghost, and then the beginning logs where she's vocalizing like a stage character had me rolling my eyes. But it grew and twisted on me, kept me off my toes. I liked the sadness of it, the idea that this thing doesn't even know what it is if it isn't an all-powerful ghost. And then that last bit, the line about vivisection actually made me recoil, it's just such an incredible violation.

I liked it, and I liked that it sneakily introduced a new rogue entity. Some people had criticisms about different elements, but my understanding of it actually kind of negated them for different reasons. I see the reveal of the other entity as proof that she was "made" and could be any number of things besides a ghost. This is why sedatives work, why containment is easy, and why the rogue guy freaks me out. Maybe he's out there doing something horrible to people that leaves them as psychic husks trying to attack others.

Also, I like the idea of incorporeal containment and standardization and I hope we see more of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh oh oh, I like this. A few people have made some fair critiques, and I still like this. A lot. +1
